title: NOC 93200 – Aircraft assemblers and aircraft assembly inspectors | GTR Immigration
description: Canada’s aerospace industry is soaring in 2026. As the third-largest global aerospace hub, Canada requires a constant influx of Aircraft Assemblers and Inspectors (NOC 93200) to meet record-breaking orders for sustainable aviation and defense aircraft. This role offers international workers the chance to build the future of flight in one of the world's most innovative sectors.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category3Major Group93 – Central control and process operators and aircraft assemblersSub-Major Group932 – Aircraft assemblers and aircraft assembly inspectors

duties: Assembling and fitting prefabricated parts to manufacture fixed-wing or rotary-wing aircraft.
Installing systems such as hydraulics, flight controls, and fuel lines.
Inspectors: Using precision measuring and testing equipment to ensure adherence to engineering specifications and safety standards.
Documenting quality control checks and maintaining detailed assembly logs.
2026 Update: Utilizing Augmented Reality (AR) headsets for real-time assembly guidance and digital blueprint overlays.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school. A College Diploma in Aviation/Aeronautical Technology is highly required for inspectors and senior assemblers.
Training: 3–6 months of on-the-job and classroom training provided by the employer.
Certification: Certification from the Canadian Council for Aviation & Aerospace (CCAA) is not mandatory but significantly increases PR success and salary.
Experience: Inspectors typically require 3–5 years of experience as an assembler.
job_outlook: The COPS projection for NOC 93200 is "Balanced." While automation is increasing in the assembly line, the need for human oversight and precision inspection remains irreplaceable. Defense spending and the shift to "Green Aviation" are expected to create 4,500 new openings by 2033.
labour_market_demand: Quebec (Montreal): "Very Good" prospects. Montreal remains a global epicenter for aerospace manufacturing (Bombardier, Airbus).
Ontario (GTA): "Good" prospects driven by major defense contracts and business jet production.
Manitoba (Winnipeg): Strong demand for composites specialists and assembly inspectors for global aerospace supply chains.
Why Work in Canada in Aerospace?
Global Prestige: Work for world-renowned companies on cutting-edge platforms like the A220 or advanced unmanned systems.
Specialized Skill Development: Access to training in advanced composites, robotics-assisted assembly, and precision avionics.
Priority Immigration: Often included in Category-Based Express Entry draws under the "Transport" or "STEM" categories.
Quality of Life: High-wage roles often located in vibrant urban centers like Montreal and Toronto.
pathways: Express Entry (Category Draws): Prioritized under the Transport Category for 2026.
Quebec Experience Program (PEQ): An excellent pathway for those working in Montreal's aerospace sector.
Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP): Highly sought after in Manitoba’s Skilled Worker Stream and Ontario’s Foreign Worker Stream.
